examples.title: Test-and-Set Lock Implementation
examples.description: Implementation of mutual exclusion using atomic Test-and-Set instruction
examples.explanation: The Test-and-Set instruction atomically returns the old value of lock while setting it to TRUE. The while loop spins (busy waits) until the lock was FALSE (unlocked). When a process exits the critical section, it sets lock back to FALSE, allowing another waiting process to acquire it. This ensures mutual exclusion.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for acquiring lock in best case, O(n) worst case where n processes compete

examples.title: Semaphore Implementation
examples.description: Classic semaphore implementation with wait and signal operations
examples.explanation: The wait operation decrements the semaphore. If result is negative, the process blocks itself and is added to the queue. The signal operation increments and wakes a process if there are waiting processes (when value ≤ 0). This is the classic definition where negative values indicate number of waiting processes.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for both operations (excluding queue operations)
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n is max number of processes that can wait

examples.title: Peterson's Solution for Two Processes
examples.description: Software solution to critical section problem for two processes
examples.explanation: Peterson's solution uses two shared variables: 'turn' for tie-breaking and 'flag' array to indicate interest. A process sets its flag to true, gives turn to the other, then waits while the other process wants to enter AND it's the other's turn. This satisfies mutual exclusion, progress, and bounded waiting for two processes. Works only for 2 processes and assumes atomic memory reads/writes.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) - bounded waiting time in CS
